content:
# James Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 262 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JAS_001_page_001.jpg

**1** From James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ — to the twelve tribes scattered across the world: Greetings.

**2** My brothers and sisters, consider it pure joy when you find yourselves surrounded by trials of every kind, **3** because you know that when your faith is tested, it produces the ability to endure. **4** But let that endurance finish its work in you, so that you may become mature and complete, not lacking anything.

**5** If any of you lacks wisdom, ask God for it — the One who gives generously to everyone without finding fault — and it will be given to you. **6** But you must ask with confidence, without doubting, because the person who doubts is like a wave of the sea, blown and tossed by the wind. **7** That person should not expect to receive anything from the Lord — **8** they are divided in their loyalties, unstable in everything they do.

**9** Believers who are poor should take pride in their high standing, **10** and those who are rich should take pride in their low standing, because they will fade away like a wildflower. **11** For the sun rises with its scorching heat and withers the plant; its blossom falls and its beauty is destroyed. In the same way, the wealthy will wither away even in the midst of their pursuits.

**12** Blessed is the person who stands firm under trial, because after enduring the test, that person will receive the crown of life that God has promised to those who love him. **13** When someone is tempted, they should never say, "God is tempting me," for God cannot be tempted by evil, and he himself tempts no one. **14** Rather, each person is tempted when they are dragged away and lured by their own desire. **15** Then desire conceives and gives birth to sin, and sin, when it has fully grown, gives birth to death.

**16** Do not be deceived, my dear brothers and sisters. **17** Every good gift and every perfect present comes down from above — from the Father of lights, with whom there is no shifting shadow or hint of change. **18** He chose to give us life through the word of truth, so that we would be a kind of firstfruits among all he has created.

**19** Understand this, my dear brothers and sisters: everyone should be quick to listen, slow to speak, and slow to become angry, **20** because human anger does not produce the righteousness that God desires. **21** So strip away every trace of moral filth and the evil that overflows among you, and humbly receive the word planted deep within you — the word that has the power to save your lives.

**22** Do not merely listen to the word and fool yourselves — put it into practice. **23** For anyone who listens to the word but does not act on it is like someone who looks at their own face in a mirror, **24** takes a look, walks away, and immediately forgets what they look like. **25** But whoever looks intently into the perfect law that sets people free and stays with it — not forgetting what they have heard but actually doing it — that person will be blessed in everything they do.

**26** If anyone considers themselves religious yet does not keep a tight rein on their tongue, they are deceiving their own heart, and their religion is worthless. **27** The religion that God our Father considers pure and genuine is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress, and to keep yourself from being corrupted by the world.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "twelve tribes scattered across the world":** The Greek *en te diaspora* (in the dispersion) refers to Jewish believers living outside Palestine. The dynamic rendering "scattered across the world" captures the lived experience of displacement and exile that the original audience would have immediately understood.

**Verse 2 — "consider it pure joy":** Sinaiticus reads *pasan charan* (all joy). The word *pasan* is rendered "pure" rather than the wooden "all" to capture the intensity — James is not saying "some joy" but urging a total reorientation of how trials are perceived.

**Verse 4 — "mature and complete, not lacking anything":** Three Greek terms — *teleioi* (mature/perfect), *holokleeroi* (whole/complete), *en meedeni leipomenoi* (lacking in nothing). This triad hammers the same point from three angles: endurance is meant to produce a fully formed person.

**Verse 8 — "divided in their loyalties":** The Greek *dipsuchos* (double-souled) is a striking word, possibly coined by James. "Divided in their loyalties" captures the sense of a person pulled in two directions simultaneously, rather than the more abstract "double-minded."

**Verse 11 — "even in the midst of their pursuits":** The Greek *en tais poreiais autou* (in his journeys/goings) likely refers to business ventures or daily affairs. The dynamic rendering emphasizes that wealth offers no protection even while one is actively engaged in accumulating it.

**Verse 15 — "desire conceives and gives birth":** James uses a vivid birth metaphor — *epithumia sullabusa* (desire having conceived) — creating a genealogy of destruction: desire begets sin, sin begets death. The translation preserves this biological imagery.

**Verse 17 — "Father of lights, with whom there is no shifting shadow":** Sinaiticus reads *parallagee ee tropes aposkiasma* — this astronomical metaphor refers to celestial bodies that cast shifting shadows. God, unlike the sun or moon, has no dark phase and no eclipse.

**Verse 18 — "a kind of firstfruits":** The term *aparcheen* (firstfruits) comes from the agricultural and sacrificial world — the first portion of the harvest offered to God. James reverses the image: God's people are his firstfruits, the leading edge of his new creation.

**Verse 21 — "the word planted deep within you":** The Greek *emphuton logon* (implanted/innate word) suggests something organic and living — not an external rule imposed from outside but a seed already growing within the believer.

**Verse 25 — "the perfect law that sets people free":** The phrase *nomon teleion ton tees eleutherias* (the perfect law of freedom) is rendered dynamically to highlight the paradox James intends: true law does not enslave but liberates.

**Verse 27 — "corrupted by the world":** The Greek *aspilon heauton teerein apo tou kosmou* (to keep oneself unstained from the world) is rendered as "corrupted" to convey active moral contamination rather than merely a ceremonial stain.

**Sinaiticus-specific note:** The Sinaiticus text of James 1 is generally well-preserved and closely aligns with the majority textual tradition for this chapter. Minor orthographic variants (e.g., itacistic spellings) are present but do not affect translation. The manuscript writes the text in continuous uncial script (*scriptio continua*) across four columns on this page.
